w:r.w:t: Chapter 9: The Ascent

The air, already thin and sharp at the base of Hermon, bit at Daniel’s lungs. This was the first step. A faint, earthy scent of damp rock and ancient snow filled his nostrils, a severe prelude to the climb. The ground felt cold beneath his bare feet. It was still damp from Rafa’s cleansing tears. This cold was a stark contrast to the warmth of the giant's parting words. He looked up. His single eye took in the colossal, snow-capped sentinel. It pierced the sky like a spear. The upper reaches were shrouded in a permanent, icy shroud. This icy height was a stark reminder of the goal he sought. "Where the veil is thinnest," his father had whispered. The words echoed, a new mantra. This mantra silenced the lingering ache of separation from Rafa. Rafa was the last true kindness he had known. He gripped the smooth, heavy dagger Rafa had given him. Its inner light was a faint, reassuring hum against his palm. This hum was a tangible connection to the ancient power that had once sought to escape the Flood. His walking stick was worn smooth by countless miles. It became an extension of his will. It was his only other possession. He had nothing else. No silver. No sandals. No water container. He was a man stripped bare. He had only the clothes on his back, the stick, and the dagger/Sword of Will. He carried only the resolute purpose in his heart. Like Job, he was losing not just his possessions. He was losing the very comforts and certainties that tethered others to the earth in The Snare. He was an empty cup. He was ready to be filled. He had nothing left to lose but the very essence of his striving. I must risk everything to gain the only thing that matters.

The path was steep. It wound like a serpent coiling around the mountain’s flank. Each step was a deliberate act of will. It was a push against the unseen forces that seemed to cling to the very rock. The air grew colder. It grew thinner. It began stealing the warmth from his bones. The biting chill of the raw stone seeped into his exposed soles. This was a relentless ache that climbed his shins. It stripped away the last illusion of earthly comfort. He had not gone far. But the sky, which had been a clear, indifferent blue, began to bruise. Dark, churning clouds boiled up from the horizon. They were the color of old lead and bruised fruit. They swallowed the sun whole. A low, guttural growl started in the distance. It grew swiftly into a deafening roar. The very ground shook. The air itself felt like a clenched fist. It tightened around him. This heavy, suffocating pressure seemed to squeeze the breath from his lungs.

Then, the sky ripped open. Not with rain, but with a torrent of raw, unbridled fury. The first drops struck him like flung pebbles. They were cold and sharp. The deluge quickly escalated into a torrential downpour. It seemed to seek to drown him where he stood. The wind was a furious, invisible hand. It tore at his cloak. It whipped his messy black hair across his face, blinding his single eye. Lightning, jagged as broken bones, ripped across the sky. It momentarily illuminated the mountain in blinding flashes. Each flash was followed by a thunderclap. The vibration rattled his skull. This was no mere storm. The mountain itself shuddered with the untamed roar of Lord Baal. The god was expressing a primal displeasure at Daniel's upward aim. Baal sought to cast him down with the might of a tempest. If I turn back now, I lose what Rafa gave me. I lose her sacrifice. I lose everything.

As the storm raged, a suffocating heat began to rise. It was thick and cloying. It clung to his skin. It made each breath a struggle. The air grew heavy, like the oppressive weight of a grave. It pressed down on him. It stole his strength. His limbs felt like lead. A profound lethargy seeped into his bones. Every movement became an act of monumental effort. He stumbled. His walking stick slipped on the slick rock. A wave of nausea washed over him. It left him clammy and weak. His skin prickled with a phantom fever. Chills ran through him despite the oppressive heat. His throat felt raw. It was as if he had swallowed dust and ashes. The cold of the raw stone against his bare feet intensified. It was a merciless bite. It was a constant reminder of his stripped vulnerability. The earth itself seemed to sigh. It was a deep, ancient exhalation of profound weariness. This was the very breath of Lord Mot. It was drawing him into its endless, quiet slumber.

Then came the whispers. They slithered from the shadows of the rocks. They were unseen. They were unheard by the ear. But they were felt in the deepest parts of his mind. "Turn back, little one. This path is not for you." "This strength you cling to… it is a fragile thing. It will break." "You are alone. Always alone." "What is the point of striving when all will be lost?" A sudden, inexplicable fear bloomed in his chest. It was cold and sharp. It urged him to flee. It commanded him to abandon the climb. The terror is not Baal, but becoming my father—trapped by fear. His Courage, a tight knot in his gut, felt as though it were unraveling. It was thread by agonizing thread. This left him exposed and trembling. The very air seemed to thin around his resolve. It left him vulnerable. He was a warrior without armor. The ground beneath his bare soles became treacherous. It shifted beneath his weight. Sometimes it gave a strange, soft give, like stepping onto thick moss. But then it turned slick and unstable. It was as if the very rock had dissolved into a watery mire. A disorienting mist, thick and cold, swirled up from unseen crevices. It clung to his face. He felt as though he were drowning in air. Each breath was a struggle against the suffocating dampness. The path ahead seemed to twist and contort. It was impossibly complex. It was a labyrinth designed to confuse and deter. His mind, usually sharp and clear, felt clouded. His thoughts scattered like dust in the wind. "This path… it makes no sense." "It is ill-designed." "You cannot navigate this." "You are a creature of the earth, like all others, bound to its currents." The mountain itself seemed to writhe beneath him. It was a living, chaotic entity. It was seeking to throw him from its back. It was as if the very essence of Lord Yam, the Prince of the Sea, churned the rock to mire. (The whispers, always just echoes of the reader's own unasked questions, were the first volley in the Internal War.)

Through the blurring haze of rain and fatigue, he saw them. Shimmering, ethereal figures appeared at the edges of his vision. They were beautiful beyond mortal comprehension. Their forms were sculpted from moonlight and ripe fields. They danced. Their movements were fluid and inviting. They beckoned him towards soft beds and warm hearths. They whispered promises of companionship. They offered gentle hands and soft breaths. A profound, aching Loneliness bloomed in his chest. It was a hunger for connection so intense it brought tears to his single eye. These tears mingled with the rain. This, he knew, was the deceptive allure of Lord Astarte. She was seeking to distract him with the siren call of companionship. She was twisting his natural need for connection into a torment of absence. Memories of his mother, of the family in Dothan, of the simple warmth of shared bread, flashed before him. These memories twisted into a cruel reminder of the solace he had abandoned. The very air around him seemed to whisper of missed embraces. It spoke of empty spaces in his heart. If I choose this, I will never see my mother's truth. I risk the love of the unseen world. His stomach, a hollow drum, began to gnaw at itself. It was a sharp, insistent Hunger. The craving intensified with every step. He saw visions of succulent roasted lamb. He saw fresh bread steaming from the oven. He saw ripe, bursting figs. The physical craving was overwhelming. It was a desperate, primal urge. He wanted simply to stop. He wanted to gorge himself. He wanted to fill the vast emptiness within. He wanted to be rooted to the earth by the simple, undeniable needs of the flesh. The very scent of the barren rock seemed to mock his empty belly. It was a constant reminder of what the earth withheld.

Then came the fire. It was not from the sky, but from within. It was a searing heat that consumed, not stifled. It crawled up his legs. It was an invisible blaze that scorched his skin. It left phantom blisters in its wake. He felt a deep, piercing throb in the scar of his missing eye. A dull, constant ache flared in his joints. Old wounds were flaring with new, inexplicable pain. A sense of malevolent eyes, unseen, watchful, seemed to press from the dark recesses of the rocks. They were judging his every weakness. They judged his every faltering breath. His very flesh seemed to turn against him. This was a betrayal from within. It was as if Lord Resheph, the god of plague, sought to consume him from the inside out. Each step was a battle. It was not just against the mountain. It was against unseen hands that sought to pull him down. They sought to break his spirit. They wanted to root him firmly in the earth. His strength faltered. His legs trembled. His will was a flickering candle flame in a gale.

Doubt, cold and sharp, pricked at the edges of his resolve. "Why this suffering?" "Why this endless striving for a height that offers only cold indifference?" "Turn back. Find comfort, find ease, find warmth." And from the very rock beneath his bare feet, a deep, resonant hum seemed to rise. It was a pervasive, crushing weight. It was trying to pin him. It was trying to absorb him into the ancient, immutable stone. This was the vast, silent authority of the mountain itself. It was the very power of Lord El, the High God of the Canaanites. El was demanding he submit. El demanded that he cease his upward striving. El demanded that he remain bound to their dominion. (Every choice to climb is a choice of Living Sacrifice, for the flesh will always be consumed by the fire of the spirit's ascent.) Above it all, the sun broke through the clouds. It offered no solace. It offered only a cold, indifferent glare. It was a vast, unfeeling eye that seemed to judge his every faltering step. It was a reminder of the unchanging, unyielding order of the cosmos. He was being tested, like Job. He was stripped of everything. Even the capacity for negative states was burned away by the fires of his trials. The principle of those who have nothing, from whom even more will be taken, was laid bare in his torment. This was a painful shedding of old flesh. This was a rebirth in fire.

But then, a pinpoint of light pierced the churning darkness above him. It was impossibly pure. It was unwavering. It was not the sun. It was not a star. It was something else. It called to the deepest part of him. It was a silent, insistent beckoning from The Source of All. "Up," his spirit cried. This echoed his mother’s voice from a time long past. "Always up." The dagger in his hand felt warm. It was a tiny ember of defiance against the overwhelming cold. Its inner light, once a steady hum, now pulsed erratically. It was a frantic beat against his palm. It was struggling to keep pace with his own diminishing strength. Yet it still offered a faint, defiant glow. With each agonizing step, the dagger's light dimmed further. Its warmth drained away. Its ancient power was being drawn into his own core. It was transforming from an external aid to an internal, burning will. He gritted his teeth. He dug his bare toes into the treacherous rock. He found purchase where there was none. He refused to yield. His single eye, though blurred by rain and tears, fixed on that distant, unwavering light. He was a small, defiant figure against the colossal wrath of the sky. He was a fragile vessel battling a cosmic storm. And yet, he held. He was shedding skin. He was burning away the old, the weak, the fearful. He was rising from ash. Each torment was a cleansing fire. Each pain was a lesson in the necessity of release.

The ascent became a blur of agony and stubborn refusal. The cold bit into his bare feet. The phantom fevers burned. The loneliness gnawed. The hunger clawed. He stumbled. He fell. He scraped his knees on the unforgiving rock. But always, always, he pushed himself up. He was a creature of pure, indomitable will. He was a single thread of defiance against the vast tapestry of opposition. The mountain seemed to stretch endlessly. Its summit was a cruel, distant promise. He felt a palpable pressure in the air. It was a silent roar against his very presence. It was a roar against his daring to aim higher than their domain. He would not break. He would not yield. He was a cup, now empty of all earthly desire. He was ready to be filled with something boundless. His foundation was built not on possessions. It was built on pure, upward intention. His very existence was becoming a testament to the upward aim. It was a Living Sacrifice. It was a living prayer against the downward pull.
The Summit

Finally, the slope began to ease. The wind, though still biting, lost its furious edge. The rain lessened. It became a fine, cold mist. The oppressive heat lifted. It was replaced by the crisp, clean air of the heights. He pushed through the last few meters. His legs were screaming. His lungs were burning. And then, he stood. He stood at the very top of Mount Hermon. The air was thin. It was almost painfully so. But it was exhilarating. The ground beneath his feet was bare rock. It was cold. But the chill no longer bit. Snow, ancient and undisturbed, lay in patches in sheltered crevices. This was a testament to the mountain's enduring height. It was feasible, barely, for a human to reach this summit. But only one driven by an unearthly will could do it. The sky above was no longer a tumultuous storm. It was a vast, inky canvas. It was studded with a million indifferent stars. Their light was sharp and cold. The air was thin, pure, and utterly silent. It was save for the faint whisper of the wind.

He looked around. He saw them. Not monuments. Not altars. He saw two hundred holes in the ground. They were perfectly circular. It was as if something immense had fallen from the heavens. It had punched through the very rock. They were ancient. They were scarred. Their edges were worn smooth by millennia. They looked like the mouths of forgotten wells. They led to unimaginable depths. These were the marks of things fallen long ago. These were beings that had chosen a downward path. They had left these silent, gaping wounds in the earth. The air around them felt heavy. It was a lingering echo of immense power and profound regret. This was a testament to the duality of existence. This was the necessary darkness that defines the light. Even The Source of All, in its infinite goodness, had cast away parts of itself. This was a painful but necessary shedding. It created the very contrasts that allowed for ascent. Daniel's body was utterly spent. The giant’s dagger was still clutched in his hand. Its inner light was now a faint, almost imperceptible pulse. He stumbled forward. The weight of the world, the battle with the unseen, the sheer, unrelenting effort of the climb, finally claimed him. He fell face down onto the cold, hard rock of the summit. The dagger slipped from his grasp. It lay beside him. Its once vibrant surface now seemed dull. A faint crack spiderwebbed across its hilt. This was a silent testament to the full transfer of its power. It was a final, sacrificial act. His breath caught. It was a ragged gasp that tore at his throat. Then it hitched. Finally, it stilled. His body, once a vessel of fierce will and endurance, lay broken and utterly still. Its battered form finally yielded to the profound, irreversible exhaustion. This was not sleep. This was the raw, cold quiet of an ending. This finality echoed the silent, vast indifference of the stars above. His earthly vessel, its purpose brutally served, was shed. It was a wrenching, agonizing separation. It was a painful but necessary surrender. It was a final, Willing Offering. The essence that had lived, breathed, and suffered within that fragile form was tearing free. It was a silent, desperate cry for release from the mortal coil. His right eye, the one that had been injured, remained as a lingering imprint on his ascending consciousness. It was a reminder of the physical trials he had endured.
The Dreamscape and Divine Becoming

Yet, even as the last warmth fled his fingertips, something else began. His mind was no longer bound by flesh. It was no longer tethered by pain or breath. It was already soaring. This was the Dreamscape. It was unlike any he had known. It was a realm of pure, unadulterated consciousness. There was no effort. There was no struggle. There was only an unbearable lightness. This was a poignant, almost painful freedom. This was The Essence of Ascent. He simply was moving. Up.

He shot past the moon. It was a colossal pearl now. It was not cold and indifferent. It was impossibly luminous. It was a gentle, knowing presence. He laughed. It was a soundless, joyful burst echoing through the void. This pure sound brought a fresh wave of tears to an unseen eye. These were tears for the life he was leaving. These were tears for the boundless existence he was entering. He surged past the stars. They were not distant pinpricks. They were vast, welcoming suns. Their light was warm and inviting. Their ancient eyes were smiling at him too. But their silent vastness spoke of the infinite separation from all that was human. He raced past groups of stars. He passed groups of groups of stars. Then he passed those groups of groups of groups—galaxies, clusters of galaxies, superclusters. He was leaving the very universe behind. It became a tiny, receding speck of light in the boundless expanse. He was moving beyond the physical. He was moving beyond the known. His essence was expanding. It was becoming one with the infinite. He was leaving behind every memory. He was leaving every touch. He was leaving every human connection, even the pain, in a silent, irreversible farewell.

And as he ascended, something shifted within him. The emptiness left by The Pit was gone. The stripping away of his capacity for negative states had made him a vessel. Now, that vessel was filling. It was filling with realms of being. Each realm was more real, more vibrant than the last. They were leading him further up and further in. They were pulling him away from all he had known. He rose.

Where the sticky miasma of Lust had promised false warmth, a crystalline Purity now permeated his very essence. It was a cool, clear current. It washed away all stain. It left behind a profound Fulfillment. This fulfillment sang through his being. It needed no outward object. It was the quiet knowing of perfect completeness.

His essence swelled into currents of Heavenly Abundance. This was a boundless fount for all. It was a wellspring of pure, generative energy. This realm shimmered with a soft, inner luminescence. It was like moonlight filtered through clean water. Its air tasted of dew and nascent life. It was the very breath of a reality more solid and joyous than any he had known. Yet this was also the taste of a world from which he could never return.

Higher still. The grasping void of Gluttony dissolved. The scorching heat of Anger faded into cool air. An absolute Serenity settled deep within his core. It was a Cooling Presence. It washed over him like balm. It left behind only the most gentle calm. It was a profound Healing Essence that resonated through him. It mended every unseen wound.

Then, the thorny thicket of Resentment gave way to universal Forbearance. This unfurled a seamless Connection to all things. It bound him in a silken embrace. It was a web of gentle understanding. His spirit flowed with a Smoothness that knew no friction. It knew no sharp edge. This plane was a t
